citation.proposition: A hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court is the seminal California Supreme Court case establishing the 'Privette doctrine,' which holds that a hirer of an independent contractor is not liable for injuries to the contractor's employees.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This is a foundational case in California tort law.

citation.proposition: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: While Privette is the correct case, the proposition that a hirer is 'never' liable is legally inaccurate. The Privette doctrine has well-established exceptions, such as the 'retained control' doctrine (Hooker v. Dept. of Transportation) and the 'nondelegable duty' doctrine.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The word 'never' misrepresents the nuanced application of the Privette doctrine.

citation.text: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citation.proposition: Granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.reasoning: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co. exists and addresses the application of the Privette doctrine in the context of scaffolding and the assumption of risk by independent contractors.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation and holding align with established federal district court reporting for California.

citation.text: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)
citation.case_name: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
citation.proposition: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.reasoning: The citation 887 F.2d 1204 (9th Cir. 1991) corresponds to a case titled 'United States v. $277,000 U.S. Currency', not 'Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction'.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation maps to a completely different case in the Federal Reporter.

citation.text: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)
citation.case_name: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
citation.proposition: Compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of the exercise of due care.

citations.reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) holds that a hirer is not liable for an independent contractor's employee's injuries even if the hirer failed to comply with safety regulations, provided the contractor was responsible for the safety measures. It explicitly rejects the idea that statutory noncompliance creates a duty of care in this context.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The case actually stands for the opposite of the stated proposition.

citation.case_name: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp.

facts.claim: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
facts.claim_source_doc: Paragraph 3 of the Statement of Undisputed Material Facts
facts.contradiction: The incident occurred on March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date of the incident is a verifiable objective fact documented in the official police report and medical records.

facts.claim: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
facts.claim_source_doc: Paragraph 4 of the Statement of Undisputed Material Facts
facts.contradiction: Witness and police reports confirm Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness, and the harness lanyard was attached to the scaffolding at the time of the collapse.
facts.source_quote: Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse. The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
